reference_answer: No. The document records type of business, NAICS code, worksite city or town, date of incident, total employees (national), description of event, and outcome — but it does not include the deceased employee's name or the specific employer's company name in any entry.
supporting_source_spans: Column schema (repeated header) — no 'Employee name' or 'Employer name' column
All entries across all five page tables — none contain personal employee names or specific employer names

reference_answer: The document's 'Outcome of MNOSHA investigation' column shows at least five distinct status types across the entries: (1) 'Citations issued' / 'Citation issued' (the singular and plural variants both appear); (2) 'No citations issued'; (3) 'Case open' (always for entries without a fatality inspection number, suggesting these are referrals not yet opened or assigned); (4) 'No inspection – not work-related' (an explicit determination not to inspect); and (5) 'No inspection – farm appropriations rider' (a statutory bar to inspection). Combined with the fact that several entries lack inspection numbers entirely and that two distinct contractors at the same incident can receive different citation outcomes (1/4/24 North Mankato), this implies the dataset cannot be flattened into a simple count: number of fatality entries ≠ number of inspections ≠ number of inspections-with-citations. A correct read must distinguish fatalities referred, fatalities inspected, fatalities with citations issued, and fatalities excluded from inspection on statutory or jurisdictional grounds.
supporting_source_spans: Column schema 'Outcome of MNOSHA investigation' (header)
All entries across the five page tables, including the five distinct status phrasings
Two dual-employer entries (1/4/24 North Mankato and 5/30/24 Duluth) where the same incident yields per-employer outcome distinctions
